advantages: Convex problems admit strong guarantees: local minima are global, duality theory applies, and gradient methods have well-understood convergence.
Convexity checks (via Hessian or specialized structure) give a clear certificate that first-order optimization is trustworthy.
Non-convex models (deep nets) can represent far richer functions, enabling state-of-the-art performance impossible with purely convex formulations.
Understanding saddles and local minima clarifies why practical tricks (momentum, noise, restarts) help real training even without global guarantees.
disadvantages: Convex formulations can be too restrictive—many powerful Gen AI models are intentionally non-convex.
Verifying convexity for complicated compositions is hard; a single indefinite Hessian direction breaks the certificate.
Non-convex optimization lacks universal guarantees: the same code can succeed or fail depending on initialization and hyperparameters.
Saddle points in high dimensions are easy to misread as convergence because the gradient is zero even though you are not at a minimum.
